name: Cíjara Reservoir Loop
name_original: Embalse de Cíjara a los pies de los Montes de Toledo — vuelta por las tierras del Rincón de Anchuras
description: This difficult 54.6-mile road cycling route around Embalse de Cijara offers challenging terrain and scenic views in the Montes de Toledo.
region_intro: Road cycling around Alía, particularly in its Spanish regions like Extremadura and Alija Del Infantado, offers diverse landscapes for cyclists. The terrain features mostly well-paved roads with varying elevation gains, ranging from moderate to challenging. This area is characterized by natural parks, river valleys, and cultural landmarks, providing a mix of scenic and physically engaging routes. While Alía in Sicily also has cycling options, its unpaved segments are less suited for dedicated road cycling. Best road cycling routes around Alía The most popular road cycling route is Pico Villuercas Loop from Guadalupe, a 38.0 miles (61.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 59 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through the Villuercas mountains. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Carrascalejo from Guadalupe – circular through the Parque Natural Geoparque Villuercas-Ibores Jara, a difficult 69.8 miles (112.4 km) path. This route traverses the natural park, offering varied terrain and extensive views. Local road cyclists also love the Charco de la Nutria from Guadalupe – circular via Castillo de Cabañas and Castañar de Ibor, a 76.2 miles (122.6 km) trail leading through natural parkland and past historic sites, often completed in about 6 hours 50 minutes. Road cycling around Alía is defined by well-paved roads, varying elevation gains, and routes through natural parks and cultural landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Alía are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 6 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Alía's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Alía?
A: Road cycling routes in the Spanish regions of Alía, particularly Extremadura and Alija Del Infantado, feature mostly well-paved roads with varying elevation gains. You'll encounter diverse landscapes, from river valleys to mountainous areas, offering both moderate and challenging sections. The region is characterized by natural parks and cultural landmarks, providing a mix of scenic and physically engaging rides.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for a moderate challenge?
A: Yes, Alía offers routes for various fitness levels. While many routes are challenging, you can find moderate options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058346182/guadalupe-from-alia-circular-by-la-calera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalupe from Alía – circular by La Calera&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 40.8 km route with a manageable elevation gain, perfect for those seeking a less strenuous ride.

Q: What are some of the more challenging road cycling routes in the Alía area?
A: For experienced cyclists, Alía presents several demanding routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058550122/pico-villuercas-loop-from-guadalupe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Villuercas Loop from Guadalupe&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 61.2 km trail with significant elevation gain through the Villuercas mountains. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058517030/carrascalejo-from-guadalupe-circular-through-the-parque-natural-geoparque-villuercas-ibores-jara" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Carrascalejo from Guadalupe&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 112.4 km path traversing the natural park with extensive views.

Q: Can I find circular road cycling routes around Alía?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Alía are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058550122/pico-villuercas-loop-from-guadalupe" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Villuercas Loop from Guadalupe&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992993881/cijara-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cíjara Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, both offering comprehensive tours of their respective areas.

Q: What kind of natural features or viewpoints can I expect to see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region around Alía is rich in natural beauty. Cyclists can enjoy stunning views from spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4253512" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint at the Estrecho de Peña Amarilla&lt;/a&gt; or reach high points like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3406495" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Villuercas Summit&lt;/a&gt;. The routes often traverse natural parks, offering diverse ecosystems and scenic vistas.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, road cycling in Alía often takes you past culturally significant sites. For example, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1058340185/charco-de-la-nutria-from-guadalupe-circular-via-castillo-de-cabanas-and-castanar-de-ibor" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charco de la Nutria from Guadalupe&lt;/a&gt; leads past historic sites like Castillo de Cabañas. The region's connection to Pilgrim Ways to the Monastery of Guadalupe also suggests a rich historical context along many paths.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Alía?
A: The road cycling routes in Alía are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 6 reviews. Cyclists often praise the well-paved roads, the varying elevation gains that provide a good challenge, and the beautiful scenery through natural parks and cultural landscapes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Alía?
A: Based on the region's characteristics, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling in Alía. Temperatures are milder, and the natural landscapes are vibrant. Summers can be hot, especially in the Spanish regions, while winters might bring cooler temperatures, though many routes remain accessible.

Q: Are there any specific mountain passes that road cyclists can tackle in the area?
A: Yes, the Alía region features several mountain passes that provide challenging climbs and rewarding descents for road cyclists. Notable passes include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2606458" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Vicente Pass&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4253507" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Arrebatachacas&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4949537" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado del Hospital&lt;/a&gt;, offering varied gradients and scenic views.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Alía?
A: There are over 25 dedicated road cycling routes around Alía, offering a wide range of distances and difficulties. This includes 10 moderate routes and 18 difficult routes, ensuring options for various skill levels and preferences.

Q: Are there any specific natural monuments to look out for while cycling?
A: While cycling around Alía, you might encounter several natural monuments. These include the intriguing &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4461596" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Chiquita Cave&lt;/a&gt; and prominent peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7463588" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Agudo&lt;/a&gt;, which offer unique points of interest along or near the routes.
tips: The reservoir is located within the Cijara National Hunting Reserve, made up of places of great beauty within the more than 25,000 hectares of this protected area.
Helechosa de los Montes is a place with Mudejar architecture, surrounded by natural beauty and with a small hermitage dedicated to San Isidro and San Francisco.
Dam that controls the waters of the Cíjara Reservoir and from this point the García Sola Reservoir appears.

faqs.question: What is the estimated cycling time for this route?
faqs.answer: This route is estimated to take around 4 hours and 19 minutes of active cycling time to complete. However, this can vary based on your fitness level, the type of bike you use, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: While much of the route traverses areas with roads in good condition, it's crucial to note that some segments may be unpaved and difficult to ride for a typical racebike. The Montes de Toledo region generally offers beautiful slopes for training, but be prepared for varied surfaces. A gravel bike or a more robust touring setup might be more suitable for comfort and safety on all sections.

faqs.question: What type of bike is recommended for this route?
faqs.answer: Given that some segments of this route may be unpaved and challenging for a standard racebike, a gravel bike or a sturdy touring bike with wider tires would be highly recommended. While a racebike can tackle the paved sections, you might find yourself walking or struggling on the unpaved parts.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers several notable points of interest. You'll cycle past the impressive Cíjara Reservoir and its Cíjara Reservoir Dam. The charming settlement of Helechosa de los Montes is also along the way, providing a glimpse into local life.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect on this ride?
faqs.answer: You can expect spectacular scenery throughout the ride. The route is set within the extensive Cijara National Hunting Reserve, known for its great natural beauty. The Montes de Toledo provide picturesque views and a sense of wilderness, with multiple viewpoints offering expansive vistas of the reservoir and surrounding landscapes.

faqs.question: Is the Reserva Regional de Caza del Cíjara subject to any special regulations or permits for cyclists?
faqs.answer: As this route passes through the Reserva Regional de Caza del Cíjara, it's advisable to check for any specific regulations regarding cycling, especially off-road or in sensitive areas. Generally, cycling on established roads and trails is permitted, but always respect local signage and environmental protection rules. No specific cycling permits are typically required for general transit on public roads within the reserve, but hunting season might bring additional considerations.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While no specific trailhead parking is detailed, the area around the Embalse de Cijara, particularly near settlements like Helechosa de los Montes or the Cíjara Reservoir Dam, would be logical starting points with potential parking options. It's recommended to check local maps or satellite views for suitable parking areas before your visit.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route would generally be during spring (April-May) or autumn (September-October). These seasons offer more moderate temperatures, making the significant distance and elevation gain more manageable. Summers can be very hot in this region, and winters might bring colder, potentially wet conditions.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily a road cycling path, and dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, specific regulations for dogs within the Cijara National Hunting Reserve should be considered. If you plan to bring a dog, it's best to keep them leashed, especially in natural protected areas, to ensure their safety and respect wildlife.

faqs.question: Does this route follow any official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route intersect with or follow parts of several official trails. You will encounter segments of the Camino de Cabañeros - Etapa 3, Camino Natural del Guadiana, Camino de Cabañeros - Etapa 2, Camino de la Cañada de la Cuadra del Membrillar a Horcajo de los Montes, and the Camino de Anchuras a Bohonal.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to resupply along the way?
faqs.answer: The region, particularly the 'lands of Rincon de Anchuras,' is known for its low population density, suggesting fewer amenities directly on the route. However, the settlement of Helechosa de los Montes is a highlight along the way and may offer opportunities for cafes or basic resupply. It's always wise to carry sufficient water and snacks, as services might be sparse between villages.
